That data is increasingly valuable as many businesses move their oper - ations into public and private clouds. Preventing Ransomware attacks Ransomware prevention begins with following best practices and developing mature security capabilities. Foundational capabilities such as establishing secure baselines, patching vulnerable software, and mature configuration management practices are crucial to prevention. Observability platforms and well-tested disas - ter recovery capabilities are vital to minimizing the impact and recovery time. Having regular security assessments, vulnerability scanning, and penetration test - ing as part of your ongoing strategy is essential to prevent a ransomware attack proactively. Understanding your current security posture e.g. controls in place to limit a successful social engineering attack and mitigating critical unpatched vulnerabilities that could be compromised will be crucial to avoiding the worst impacts of a ransomware attack. When malware reaches the encryption stage, very little can be done to prevent your devices from being impacted.
====================================================================================================

Chunk 1140
source_id: cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper
page_key: community_resources_security_whitepaper_v2_cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper_may2022_v2_pdf
page_number: 36.0
chunk_id: cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper__community_resources_security_whitepaper_v2_cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper_may2022_v2_pdf__page_0036__chunk_0003
doc_id: cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper__community_resources_security_whitepaper_v2_cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper_may2022_v2_pdf__page_0036
chunk_index: 3
sentence_count: 5
word_count: 118
char_count: 836
text:
Averting ransomware events from occurring, needs malware detection in earlier phases of the MITRE ATT&CK framework. To accomplish this, relying solely on signature-based detection capabilities and indi - cator-based threat intelligence is not a complete solution. Enterprises will need to perform defense in depth strategies that include micro-segmentation and behavior - al analysis for internal and cloud network segments and any external related traffic. Developing a secure software factory and deployment pipeline will significantly reduce the risk of ransomware by reducing the attack surface by controlling the number of deployed vulnerabilities and mandatory code/configuration manage - ment. A software factory is ideal for implementing code scanning, image scan - ning, code review, and validating the supply chain provenance.
====================================================================================================

Chunk 1141
source_id: cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper
page_key: community_resources_security_whitepaper_v2_cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper_may2022_v2_pdf
page_number: 37.0
chunk_id: cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper__community_resources_security_whitepaper_v2_cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper_may2022_v2_pdf__page_0037__chunk_0001
doc_id: cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper__community_resources_security_whitepaper_v2_cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper_may2022_v2_pdf__page_0037
chunk_index: 1
sentence_count: 11
word_count: 156
char_count: 1095
text:
Risk is further reduced by treating configuration changes as code that must tra - verse through the secure software factory, including scanning and code reviews. Configuration management can be tracked through the pipeline and audited by an external observability platform. Anomalies, including administrative actions rarely performed, must be identified and tracked. Expected anomalies should be tracked and labeled for auditing pur - poses. The observability platform should tag unexpected anomalies for additional review. Rule engines and AI/ML may automate some anomaly detection for scal - ability, but automated detection should not yet replace humans who can reason about more complicated scenarios. Deployments must follow the Principle of Least Privileges. This principle is critical to reducing the blast radius of a compromised deployment. Operators should iso - late databases from the workloads, with minimal privileges allowed. Best practic - es include using views, prepared statements, disabling updates/deletes when not needed. Backups should be maintained and regularly tested.
====================================================================================================

Chunk 1142
source_id: cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper
page_key: community_resources_security_whitepaper_v2_cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper_may2022_v2_pdf
page_number: 37.0
chunk_id: cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper__community_resources_security_whitepaper_v2_cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper_may2022_v2_pdf__page_0037__chunk_0002
doc_id: cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper__community_resources_security_whitepaper_v2_cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper_may2022_v2_pdf__page_0037
chunk_index: 2
sentence_count: 9
word_count: 139
char_count: 914
text:
For more advanced protection, enable ledger capabilities of the underlying storage and database, such as object versioning. Protecting data encryption keys is also vital. A ransomed cryptographic key can be just as devastating as ransomed raw data. Production systems with sensitive data should store their keys in KMS or HSM. Cloud environments offer high-quali - ty KMS services that are FIPS 140-2 certified. Finally, it is essential to limit the paths of communication between systems. Op - erators can do this in a few ways. If you are practicing Zero Trust, you can ensure only systems with approved and valid cryptographic identities are capable of communicating through encrypted channels such as Mutual TLS. For applications unaware of cryptographic identities, it is essential to establish encrypted tunnel network policies and provision next-generation firewalls to protect against mali - cious attacks.
====================================================================================================

Chunk 1143
source_id: cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper
page_key: community_resources_security_whitepaper_v2_cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper_may2022_v2_pdf
page_number: 37.0
chunk_id: cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper__community_resources_security_whitepaper_v2_cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper_may2022_v2_pdf__page_0037__chunk_0003

chunk_index: 3
sentence_count: 2
word_count: 62
char_count: 362
text:
Ideally, steps taken to prevent a ransomware attack work as expected, in keeping an organization away from being a victim of a successful attack. However, these measures take time to implement though, and while they should make an orga - nization harder to compromise and more able to recover from attack, it is not full proof and there are never any guarantees.
====================================================================================================

text:
Ransomware Incident Response As per NIST Incident Response Guide, these steps are involved in managing a ransomware incident: Preparation Establish an incident response plan that has gone through several rigorous table - top exercises with your team to understand how your organization will respond to a potential Ransomware attack. This will include whom to contact if your orga - nization is a victim of a Cyberattack. If applicable, this will include emergency contact numbers in your organization, carrier, breach coach, DFIR company, and MSP/MSSP. You will need to activate the team as quickly as possible to start the next stage in the life cycle. Detection & Analysis In this stage, you will want to quickly and efficiently detect the suspicious/mali - cious to contain and eradicate it. In this process, you will need to do your best to maintain digital forensics evidence as much as possible.
====================================================================================================

text:
This way, you can inves - tigate the incident to find artifacts that will provide crucial information on how the threat actor compromised your IT infrastructure/cloud environment. You will also want to know if they moved laterally throughout the environment and what data the threat actor gained access to. Through this stage, you will also if you do not already have an Endpoint Detection and Response solution in place, you will want to deploy one as quickly as possi - ble. This will give you visibility to your endpoints to detect, quarantine, or kill any suspicious or malicious activity. This way, you can start working on containing the active threats. Containment, Eradicate & Recovery Working on containment is essential to eradicate the active threats so that your team can start working on recovering from the Cyberattack. Containment could be disconnecting the endpoint(s) that have been identified as compromised from the network without shutting these systems off.
====================================================================================================

This is essential because Threat Actors are known to hold organizations hostage and request greater demands when they realize they still have control of the environment. As soon as you feel comfortable that you have contained the active threat, it appears to be eradicated from your IT/Cloud environment. You will now start working on recovery. This next point is crucial to responding to a Ransomware attack and could save you millions of dollars. It is essential to have a backup program that has been tested and protects your backups. It is recommended that you have an off-site backup solution for all your critical systems and data. These backups should be scanned for malware and stored in a secure location. These backups will be essential to restoring business continuity and not having to negotiate payment with a Threat Actor for your data, potentially.

text:
Post-Incident Retrospective Here is where a team debriefs after a Ransomware attack to understand the successes and challenges that occurred through the incident. This is a great time to evaluate the Incident Response Plan, Administrative and Technical Controls, Disaster Recovery Plans, Back-ups, Endpoints, Change Management, External and Internal Communication plans in case of breach. Having this new insight into having gone through a Ransomware attack will change how a business thinks about its operations and day-to-day activities. This should not be short-lived, and this new understanding needs to be implemented into existing business practice and security program.
====================================================================================================

Security Principles Secure Defaults (New in v2) A strong security system in its default state is possible, cost effective and transparent. Building or transitioning towards such a system involves following these guidelines in cloud native context: 1. Make security a design requirement 2. Applying secure configuration has the best user experience 3. Selecting insecure configuration is a conscious decision 4. Transition from insecure to secure state is possible 5. Secure defaults are inherited 6. Exception lists have first class support 7. Secure defaults protect against pervasive vulnerability exploits 8. Security limitations of a system are explainable For more details on these guidelines, please refer to this page: Secure Defaults: Cloud Native 8 Least Privilege Least privilege is just as important, or perhaps the most important, aspect of cloud native architectures, and must be considered at all parts of the stack where an authentication or authorization decision is made.
====================================================================================================

Chunk 1151
source_id: cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper
page_key: community_resources_security_whitepaper_v2_cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper_may2022_v2_pdf
page_number: 40.0
chunk_id: cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper__community_resources_security_whitepaper_v2_cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper_may2022_v2_pdf__page_0040__chunk_0002
doc_id: cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper__community_resources_security_whitepaper_v2_cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper_may2022_v2_pdf__page_0040
chunk_index: 2
sentence_count: 6
word_count: 124
char_count: 825
text:
Traditionally Least Privilege has been thought of at the account layer whether that account is a human or a service. In cloud native, least privilege must be applied at every layer of the stack. It should also be considered when evaluating the specific tooling responsible for fulfilling each layer’s execution. Organizations may find, as they explore various products and capabilities, that many containers have privileged-by-default deployments or required root privileges to operate. As a result, additional measures may need to be taken to isolate those elevated privileges from the rest of the workload. Organizations should consider all areas to employ isolation and least privilege in their workloads and deployments; from cgroups and system calls in the runtime environment to artifact management and rootless builds.
====================================================================================================

executing in a given environment. Rootless services and containers are vital to ensuring that if an attacker does get into an organization’s environment, they cannot easily traverse between the container they gain access to and the underlying host or containers on other hosts. Mandatory Access Control (MAC) implementations (e.g. SELinux and AppArmor) can limit the privileges beyond those set to the container or namespace. Additionally, they provide container isolation at the host level to prevent container breakout or pivoting from one container to another, to escalate privileges beyond those permitted by the access control in place. Roles and Responsibilities When moving to cloud native architectures and deployments, organizations should expect to see adjustments in legacy security roles and responsibilities and create new security roles specific to the cloud.
====================================================================================================

Chunk 1154
source_id: cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper

page_number: 41.0
chunk_id: cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper__community_resources_security_whitepaper_v2_cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper_may2022_v2_pdf__page_0041__chunk_0002

chunk_index: 2
sentence_count: 6
word_count: 178
char_count: 1215
text:
With the rapid onset of modern development methodologies and better alignment of IT activities with business needs, security must be adaptive, commensurately applied with actual risk, and transparent. It is unreasonable to expect developers and operations to become security experts. Security practitioners need to partner with developers, operations, and other project life elements to make security and compliance enforcement fully integrated with process modernization efforts and development lifecycles. Doing so means findings are reported in real-time through the tools in use by developers for habitual resolution, akin to how build failures are resolved at notice. The blurred lines that often occur in DevOps environments should not replace clear separation of duties (SoD) when it comes to managing security in cloud native environments. While developers will be a lot more involved in implementing and executing security measures, they do not set policy, need not gain visibility into areas that aren’t required for their role, etc. - this separation should be implemented between roles and across product and application teams in accordance with the organization’s risk tolerance and business practices.
====================================================================================================

Ultimately, allowing for reorganization roles to be reassigned to new individuals without increasing scope of access with the new assignment. Organizations will need to reevaluate their asset risks as products and services migrate to the cloud. With ownership and management changes of the technology in use and its deployment stack, executives should expect significant risk posture changes. Shared responsibility between providers and teams

Supply Chain Security (New in v2) The security of a system is only as good as the supply chain that it relies on. Defending the supply chain requires securing how software and hardware are designed, implemented, distributed, configured, stored, and verified. An effective supply chain strategy relies on mature policies and procedures to mitigate risks associated with first and third-party software creators, integrators, and distributors. Each party must communicate relevant information accurately and effectively. A supply chain policy should contain diverse providers with con - trols designed to limit harm from a compromised supply chain. It provides prove - nance of systems, software, and configurations with the ability to trace the origin and validate the integrity of both the artifact and the chain that produced it. The software supply chain consists of source code, secondor third-party code, build pipelines, artifacts, and deployments.
====================================================================================================

Chunk 1157
source_id: cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper

char_count: 894
text:
Each of these stages must be performed by an authenticated trusted party such that it can be verified cryp - tographically and automated where possible. All trusted entities should have limited authorization scope to reduce the impact of a compromise. Software Bill of Materials (SBOMs) are a critical first step towards discover - ing what software components you have so then you may correlate them with known vulnerabilities. SBOMs should be generated using standardized formats such as but not limited to SPDX, Cyclone DX, SWID, at build-time from the source code and should link to the SBOM of imported libraries and tools.The source code and component metadata included in the SBOM may also be used by developers and operators to identify tampering of the software supply chain. The CI/CD system should sign both application and container images with sig - natures reproduced in the SBOM.
====================================================================================================

Chunk 1158
source_id: cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper
page_key: community_resources_security_whitepaper_v2_cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper_may2022_v2_pdf
page_number: 42.0
chunk_id: cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper__community_resources_security_whitepaper_v2_cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper_may2022_v2_pdf__page_0042__chunk_0003
doc_id: cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper__community_resources_security_whitepaper_v2_cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper_may2022_v2_pdf__page_0042
chunk_index: 3
sentence_count: 6
word_count: 156
char_count: 944
text:
Operators may use post-build SBOM gener - ators from binaries or images to validate the accuracy of the build-time SBOM. End to end attestations may be used to validate the processes used by software creators and suppliers. These attestations should be added to every step in the software supply chain. In some cases, it may be challenging to identify the software that is impacted by CVEs and implement fixes since SBOMs can contain thousands of dependen - cies and do not identify if they have a CVE (out of scope of the SBOM). In these types of situations, generating delta reports during the build process and storing them with the corresponding SBOMs can help organizations to identify the actu - al vulnerable software (together with its version) much faster and with less effort or potential error. An additional benefit of delta reports is that they can help to accurately identify non-vulnerable but impacted (dependent) software also.
====================================================================================================

Chunk 1159
source_id: cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper
page_key: community_resources_security_whitepaper_v2_cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper_may2022_v2_pdf
page_number: 43.0
chunk_id: cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper__community_resources_security_whitepaper_v2_cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper_may2022_v2_pdf__page_0043__chunk_0001
doc_id: cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper__community_resources_security_whitepaper_v2_cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper_may2022_v2_pdf__page_0043
chunk_index: 1
sentence_count: 9
word_count: 141
char_count: 987
text:
A secure CI/CD system should generate SBOMs and attestations. Attestations should include the CI step’s process, environment, materials, and products. Evi - dence should be cryptographically verified when possible. The software producer should use trusted documents such as signed meta-data documents and signed payloads to verify the authenticity and integrity of the built environment. It’s equally important to keep track of dependencies of the CI/CD’s supply chain in this process. Suppliers should provide proof of assessments and reviews of their components and dependencies. Suppliers should provide timely notifications of vulnerabilities, whether they are affected by those vulnerabilities or breaches. Up - coming standards such as VEX will provide a common framework for exchanging information on vulnerabilities. Operators and security teams should store all of the above information in a query - able inventory system to discover vulnerable or non-compliant systems quickly.
====================================================================================================

A mature and automated SBOM, CVE, and VEX program may provide relevant in - formation to other security and compliance controls. For example, the infrastruc - ture may automatically report non-complying systems to an observability platform or deny providing a necessary cryptographic workload identity, effectively quar - antining it from compliant systems in Zero-Trust environments. The CNCF has produced the Software Supply Chain Best Practices White Pa - per to assist you with designing a secure supply chain process. This whitepaper provides more details about securing the software supply chain and discusses relevant CNCF projects that developers and operators can use to secure various stages of the supply chain. GitOps (New in v2) GitOps is code-based infrastructure and operational procedure that rely on Git as a source control system.
====================================================================================================

Chunk 1161
source_id: cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper
page_key: community_resources_security_whitepaper_v2_cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper_may2022_v2_pdf
page_number: 43.0
chunk_id: cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper__community_resources_security_whitepaper_v2_cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper_may2022_v2_pdf__page_0043__chunk_0003
doc_id: cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper__community_resources_security_whitepaper_v2_cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper_may2022_v2_pdf__page_0043
chunk_index: 3
sentence_count: 6
word_count: 160
char_count: 1044
text:
It is an evolution of Infrastructure as Code (IaC) and a DevOps best practice that leverages Git as the single source of truth, and central - ized control management for creating, updating, and deleting IT system archi - tecture. GitOps allows separating deployments from development and use full advantage of the immutable declarative infrastructure. Every element of the envi - ronment can be deployed as often as needed with the same result, instances are redeployed instead of restoring from multiple unique configurations and versions. Traditional processes mostly rely on human operational knowledge, expertise, and actions performed manually but in case of GitOps all changes are made as interac - tion with Git repository. Therefore, the Git repository and GitOps process become crucial to secure and should be secure by design. Immutability of infrastructure protects from making changes from outside the main deployment process and easier to detect and reverse environment changes based on the declarative state in the Git repository.
====================================================================================================

Chunk 1162
source_id: cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper
page_key: community_resources_security_whitepaper_v2_cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper_may2022_v2_pdf
page_number: 44.0
chunk_id: cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper__community_resources_security_whitepaper_v2_cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper_may2022_v2_pdf__page_0044__chunk_0001
doc_id: cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper__community_resources_security_whitepaper_v2_cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper_may2022_v2_pdf__page_0044
chunk_index: 1
sentence_count: 3
word_count: 114
char_count: 730
text:
Usage of IaC and GitOps increase the overall security of the infrastructure itself by limiting manual operations, providing an audit of all changes, a declarative single source of truth, policy enforcement via the necessary controls and gates on pro - cesses to ensure that security requirements are met. Using GitOps tools and tech - nologies, organizations can mitigate different vectors of attacks, i.e. by reducing the number of people and machines that have access to the target system. GitOps processes are responsible to deliver changes to the production environ - ment and if that process is compromised, then the adversary may open infrastruc - ture backdoors or may introduce harmful software to production environments.
====================================================================================================

char_count: 1033
text:
Some noteworthy guidelines to follow based on least privilege principle and sepa - ration of duties are: • Restrict access to repository and branches • Never store unencrypted credentials or secrets in the Git repository and block sensitive data being pushed to Git • Enforce strong identity with GPG Signed Commits, to give accountability and traceability • Require linear history and maintain a commit history by disallowing force pushes • Enforce branching policy, especially protect the main branch and require code review before merging • Monitor for vulnerabilities, and keep Git and GitOps tools up to date • Rotate SSH keys and Personal Access Tokens, block unauthorized access to Git repositories • Utilize a dedicated non-user technical account for access where credentials are frequently rotated and short-lived • Limit users who can elevate permissions to remove security features to cover their tracks via deletion of audit trails and silencing of alerts In summary, GitOps can enable elimination of vulnerabilities long
====================================================================================================

Zero Trust Architecture Zero trust architectures mitigate the threats of lateral movement within a network through finegrained segmentation, micro-perimeters, and removing implicit trust to data, assets, applications, and services (DAAS) with verification and enforce - ment policies. Most common implementations of zero-trust architecture rely on cryptographic concepts to create zero trust. This is primarily based on the ability to have specific key material secured in hardware or tokens and managed in a way
====================================================================================================

Chunk 1165
source_id: cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper
page_key: community_resources_security_whitepaper_v2_cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper_may2022_v2_pdf
page_number: 45.0
chunk_id: cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper__community_resources_security_whitepaper_v2_cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper_may2022_v2_pdf__page_0045__chunk_0001

chunk_index: 1
sentence_count: 6
word_count: 158
char_count: 969
text:
where they can be securely communicated to a platform. The foundational building block that zero trust architecture usually consists of several aspects: • Each entity can create proof of whom the identity is • Entities can independently authenticate other identities (i.e. Public Key Infrastructure) • Communications between entities remain confidential and untampered The zero trust framework creates the zero trust building blocks by leveraging a strong root of trust: the ability to tie a tamper-resistant trust to an entity or pro - cess is the foundational building block. It then requires attestations: the ability to attest, validate, and prove the identity of an entity. For the example of container services, how do I check that this container is who it claims to be. This needs to be verified with the orchestrator, but to trust the orchestrator, we need to ensure it is running untampered, which can only be ensured if we are running a trusted OS, BIOS, etc.
====================================================================================================

Attestations are usually a chain as well. Zero trust also requires secure communication between entities. While network segmentation provides value to zero trust architectures and should be considered, is not an end all solution to zero trust implementation. Orchestrator network poli - cies as well as use of a service mesh are all components of a comprehensive zero trust solution. More information on zero trust concepts is available widely online. Security Stack (New in v2) Implementation of these security assurances across the four lifecycle phases are explored in depth in Cloud native security map that can be found here: https:// cnsmap.netlify.app. The one side effect of security implemented through these tools across the stack is that they help in compliance needs of a cloud native environment. Compliance Designing a system with the appropriate set of security controls that address regulatory, and compliance guidance makes cloud native resources more secure.

Adoption and implementation of these benchmarks enable teams to test for a hardened baseline and deploy secure-by-default workloads. However, they can - not consider data flows and custom usage of the platforms under testing. Security practitioners should implement them as a guide rather than a checklist. Regulatory Audits Many financial, health, government, and other entities need to comply with a specific set of requirements to protect the system. The users trust the systems to keep their interactions private and secure. Every organization should evalu - ate which regulatory standards apply to them (e.g., PCI-DSS, HIPAA, FedRAMP, GDPR, etc.). They should then determine how specific requirements apply to their cloud native systems and how they will implement those standards’ real-world implementation. This evidence-gathering mechanism supporting adherence to specific standards should be automated with non-repudiation guarantees when - ever possible. Personas and Use Cases The focus is on security, protection, detection, and auto-response wherever possible.

Industries Enterprise Core areas of concern for Enterprise to adopt a cloud native model are maintain - ing the current process and procedures while meeting the business objective. Keeping the interoperability, data loss or leakage, and security risk exposure at a min - imum when new standards and practices are introduced throughout the organization. Microbusiness Core areas of concern for Small businesses to adopt a cloud native model are the ability to focus on short term goals and foster innovation to meet intense compe - tition. The lack of resources, budget, technology depth, and best practice hinders their ability to adapt to cloud native solutions. Small business requires repeatable patterns and a small IT footprint to solve the challenges.
====================================================================================================

Finance Core areas of concern for financial and insurance industries that are essential to successful cloud native adoption are legal framework compliance, data localiza - tion requirements, immutable audit logging, unauthorized disclosure of informa - tion, and fraud detection.. Fraud, being somewhat unique to the sector, can have a direct impact on fund availability, making the integrity of financial transactions of paramount importance. Healthcare Core areas of concern for healthcare industries that are essential to successful cloud native adoption are unauthorized disclosure of information, timeliness, and availability of records, and accuracy of records. Due to the nature and practices of the healthcare industry, the availability of records and their associated content is the basis by which medical decisions are made. Academia and Education Core areas of concern for educational institutions for successful cloud native adoption can be dependent upon the intended end user.
====================================================================================================

Public Sector Core areas of concern for Public Sector organizations that are essential to suc - cessful cloud native are security, data sovereignty, compliance, and vendor lockin. Additionally, timeliness of deployments and fea - tures may also be a strong consideration. The adoption of cloud native, along with modern methodologies, can increase organizational velocity, which is critical to many areas of the public sector.

Use case: Securing Financial Institutions under EU regulations (New in v2) Cloud Native architectures in public and private clouds have become the standard solution for modern IT for fast innovation, delivering more value to their custom - ers with dramatically less effort. This is a big challenge, especially for regulated sectors such as finance due to the complexity of their legacy systems, and compli - ance challenges including concerns raised by regulatory institutions. Let’s look at three main authorities in case of European Union: • EBA - The “European Banking Authority” is an independent authority that works to ensure effective and consistent prudential regulation and supervision across the EU banking sector. • EIOPA - The “European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority” is a European Union financial regulatory institution. • ESMA - The “European Securities and Markets Authority” works in the field of securities legislation and regulation to improve the functioning of financial markets in Europe, strengthening investor protection and cooperation be - tween national competent authorities.
====================================================================================================

Each of the above authorities pay particular attention to: • Flexible and secure multi-cloud strategy, • Solid foundations for portability and interoperability, • Right to access and right to audit, • Security of data, • Exit strategy, • Risk assessment and concentration risk. Risk assessment should be conducted considering the expected benefits, security, costs, business continuity, legal, compliance, operational and concentration risks. The risk assessment when performed lends itself to a properly designed solu - tion to mitigate risks and strengthen operational resilience. Additionally, financial institutions need to develop comprehensive exit plans that are documented and sufficiently tested. These plans should be updated as needed, including in case of any service changes. Cloud Native architecture helps to meet the above regulations and requirements by microservices architecture, service meshes, modern design, containers, back - end services, and a high level of automation. Microservice architecture has en - abled the loosely coupled integration of service interfaces and the interoperable

Evolution of Cloud Native Security Container technologies are a continuously evolving space with rampant adoption. The threat landscape for cloud native technologies and the corresponding secu - rity challenges in mitigating and resolving these threats evolves as well. These, in addition to a complex ecosystem for secure container platforms, require a fully formulated, well-thought-out security strategy, with technical controls and auto - mation for security policy enforcement, response, and operational discipline.

source_id: cncf_cloud_native_security_whitepaper

chunk_index: 2
sentence_count: 6
word_count: 147
char_count: 1052
text:
Containers provide enormous security benefits when appropriately implemented. They provide greater transparency, modularity, reduced attack surface, easier application components updates, and a consistent environment for application components to run. This consistency allows for parallel security to thrive in devel - opment, test, and production runtime environments. They also reduce the impact of enterprise wide security incidents when enabling proper isolation built between applications (essentially enabling micro segmentation in enterprises which may have a flat network) as part of a layered defense-in-depth security strategy. With all the current challenges in security, the number of security tools needed, and the shortage of skills and talent in the market, securing a container platform is a monumental challenge. We expect to see increased migration to the cloud as container service offerings by cloud providers become more mature, with more cloud native security & intelligence tooling integrated over incompatible specifi - cations.
====================================================================================================

As these attacks become more prevalent, more intricate, and expand, cloud native security has to evolve to put a more significant focus on enterprises and

It is critical that organizations at the brink of mature adoption earnestly analyze and apply core security concepts to alleviate the lag in hardening and environ - mental control for Day 2 operations. While security-specific guidance and controls may not yet exist for most inno - vations we see today and coming in the future, core security concepts in cloud native architectures can be consistently applied while designing, developing, and deploying new capabilities. These core security concepts are: • Protection from unauthorized access (person and non-person entities) - Ephemerality reduces asset exposure to unauthorized entities by consistently rebasing from a known good state. • Immutability to preserve the integrity of content and code. • Availability of services, tooling, and content - Distributed nature of cloud na - tive architectures provides resilience and redundancy. • Auditing and Accountability - Ensure that detection of irregularities and keep - ing track of authorized changes is possible.

Appendix Learning from First Version (New in v2) Each release of the whitepaper triggers a retrospective process that assesses what worked well, what we should do more and opportunities where we can im - prove. Feedback from the first version retrospective was addressed throughout the paper. Have Feedback For Us? Dat Doan Hardening Application Deployment in Kubernetes Computer Science Prof. Tuomas Aura Msc. Jose Luis Martin Navarro 31 7 2025 69+6 English Abstract Containers and Kubernetes are widespread technologies that have evolved how applicationsaredeveloped,deployed,andmanaged,offeringportability,scalability,and resilience. However, the security challenges associated with Kubernetes deployments remaincritical,especiallygivenitspopularityforhostingmulti-tenantapplications. Severalframeworkstrytoguidethedeploymentofsecureapplications,buttheyare farfromofferingcompleteandfine-grainedinstructionsonhowtoachieveasecure cluster. This creates uncertainty about what securing a deployment requires, leaving applications susceptible to a variety of attack vectors, such as misconfigured roles, exposed secrets, and unmonitored runtime behavior. Addressing these vulnerabilities demands a systematic application of established security practices tailored to Kubernetes environments. The objective of this thesis is to deliver a robust, automated, and scalable security hardeningframeworkforaKubernetescluster. Thisthesisproposesanewapproach to implementing security practices systematically within a cloud and Kubernetes system. It covers most security measures and attack vectors while ensuring early attentiontosecuritythroughatwo-dimensionalmatrixforcontainersecuritypractices. Theimplementationincludesawiderangeofconfigurationandbestpracticesfrom standard industrial documents such as RBAC, network policy, configuration scanning, distroless image, and so on.
====================================================================================================

Dat Doan Sovellusten käyttöönoton vahvistaminen Kubernetesissa Computer Science Prof. Tuomas Aura Msc. Jose Luis Martin Navarro 31 7 2025 69+6 Abstract KontitjaKubernetesovatlaajallelevinneitäteknologioita,jotkaovatkehittäneet sovellusten kehittämistä, käyttöönottoa ja hallintaa tarjoten siirrettävyyttä, skaalautuvuutta ja vikasietoisuutta. Kubernetes-käyttöönottoon liittyvät tietoturvahaasteet ovat kuitenkinedelleenkriittisiä,erityisestiottaenhuomioonsensuosionmonivuokralaisten sovellustenylläpidossa.Useatkehyksetpyrkivätohjaamaanturvallistensovellusten käyttöönottoa, mutta ne ovat kaukana kattavista ja yksityiskohtaisista ohjeista turvallisen klusterin saavuttamiseksi. Tämä luoepävarmuuttasiitä,mitäkäyttöönoton suojaaminen vaatii, jolloin sovellukset ovat alttiita erilaisille hyökkäysvektoreille, kuten väärin määritetyille rooleille, paljastuneille salaisuuksille ja valvomattomalle ajonaikaiselle toiminnalle. Näiden haavoittuvuuksien korjaaminen edellyttää vakiintuneidentietoturvakäytäntöjensystemaattistasoveltamista,jotkaonräätälöity Kubernetes-ympäristöihin.Tämänopinnäytetyöntavoitteenaontarjotavankka,automatisoitu ja skaalautuva tietoturvan vahvistamiskehys Kubernetes-klusterille. Tämä opinnäytetyö ehdottaa uutta lähestymistapaa tietoturvakäytäntöjen systemaattiseen toteuttamiseen pilvija Kubernetes-järjestelmässä. Se kattaa useimmat turvatoimenpiteet ja hyökkäysvektorit varmistaen samalla varhaisen tietoturvan huomioimisen kaksiulotteisen matriisin avulla konttitietoturvakäytännöille. Toteutus sisältäälaajan valikoimankonfiguraatioitajaparhaitakäytäntöjäteollisuusstandardidokumenteista, kutenRBAC,verkkopolitiikka,konfiguraationskannaus,jakelutonlevykuvajaniin edelleen. Ehdotettu lähestymistapa validoidaan tapaustutkimuksen avulla, joka on linjassa DevOps-elinkaaren eri vaiheiden kanssa. Tulokset osoittavat kaksi pääkohtaa. Ensinnäkin esitetään kattavampi lähestymistapa tapaustutkimuksen avulla, jotta voidaanhelpostisaadasekäkokonaiskuvaettäyksityiskohdatKubernetesintietoturvasta. Toiseksi tutkimus käy läpi vaiheet, jotta voidaan nähdä uuden lähestymistavan toteutettavuus, ja osoittaa sen tehokkuus ohjelmiston ja riippuvuuksien vakavien ja kriittisten haavoittuvuuksien 100% vähentämisessä. Tämä työ auttaa parantamaankonttisovellustenyleistätietoturvatilannetta.Menetelmätarjoaaparannuksen Kubernetes-klusterin tietoturvan toteutuksen tehokkuuteen. Kubernetes, Turvallisuus, Containerization, Docker

1 Introduction In recent years, cloud-based applications have been developed with the support of various frameworks and technologies. Kubernetes and Docker are the mainstream invirtualizationtechnologywith84%usagein2023[1]. Dockerisanopen-source technology that encourages developers to build, deploy, run, update, and handle containers,andKubernetesisaportable,extensibleplatformforoperatingcontainerized applications[2,3]. Thesetechnologiessimplifythedeployment,increasethereusability andscalabilityofmicroservicessystems. Giventheirwidespreadadoption,therelated security concerns increase, for example [4]. Therefore, organizations such as OWASP and NSA research and provide their best practices to secure cloud deployments [5, 6]. The complex nature of cloud environments, which involve multiple abstraction layers and technologies, makes it harder to completely secure both applications and infrastructure. While there are tools and hardening guides available to protect Kubernetesclusters,thereisalackofacomprehensiveframework,leavingpractitioners the task of conveying all the fragmented knowledge into a list of actionable steps needed to harden the cluster and applications. The main objective of this thesis is to create a hardening framework based on previous state-of-the-art guidelines. This research combines the best existing methodstoproduceastrategytoreducethethreatexposureandlimitthenumberof vulnerabilities.
====================================================================================================

2 Background This chapter covers basic concepts and technologies related to containers and their orchestration. Section2.1coversthebasicinformationaboutcontainersandDocker. Section 2.2 describes Kubernetes, the most used technology to orchestrate containers, andthefocusofthiswork. Section2.3definesDevOps,amodernsoftwaredevelopment approach,whichistheideaforthelaterproposedmethod. Finally,section2.4introduces threatmodeling,astructuredapproachtoanalyzingpotentialthreatsinKubernetes, and several security principles to create a hardening strategy when developing a Kubernetes system. 2.1 Containers and Docker Virtualizationistheabstractionofcomputerresourcesfromphysicalhardware[7]. Before the popularity of containers and Docker, virtualization technologies were alreadycommon. Forexample, virtualmachines, the ancestorofcontainers, arean approachthatprovidesanefficient,isolatedreplicaofacomputersystem’senvironment [8]. EachvirtualmachinehasitsownOSandsystemlibraries. Popularvirtualmachine providers are Microsoft Hyper-V, VirtualBox, and VMware [9, 10, 11]. The approach ofvirtualmachinesforvirtualizationprovidesdiverse andwell-establishedsoftware environments. However, a major issue with the traditional method is the performance of resource-intensive, high storage use, and slow start-up time. In contrast, using containers is the approach of visualizing only the OS, not the wholecomputer. Eventhoughthistechnologyhasbeeninvented,itbecamepopular andanindustrialstandardonlywithDocker, aplatformthatsupportsdevelopersto createandmanagecontainerseffectivelyanddeliverthesoftwarefaster. Thecontainer isastandardsoftwareunitthatpackagescodealongwithallitsdependencies,ensuring the application runs quickly and reliably across different computing environments [12]. The containers share the OS kernel, binaries, and libraries. There are many improvementscomparedtoVM,suchasalightweightprocess,nativeperformance, and a short startup time.
====================================================================================================

text:
Figure 2: Kubernetes architecture 2.2.1 Kubernetes Architecture AKubernetesclustercomprisesa controlplane andmultiple workernodes. Thecontrol plane interacts with and manages all the worker nodes. In the control plane, there are differentcomponents: controlmanager,KubernetesAPIserver akakube-apiserver, scheduler, key-value database etcd. First, kube-apiserver’s main function is to expose the Kubernetes API for the cluster. It can be scaled horizontally using many instances to balance the workload [3]. It handles and validates all requests from worker nodes, developers, and cluster administrators [16]. Second, etcd is a consistent and highly available key-value store for the whole cluster [3]. It persistently stores internal state, metadata, secrets, and configuration data [16]. kube-apiserver is the only component that communicates and updates states in etcd. In addition, the kube-scheduler has the role of allocating a node to a Pod. TheworkernodeiswheretheapplicationsdefinedineachPodrun. Inaddition, two other Kubernetes components in the worker node are the kubeletandkube-proxy. KubeletmanagesPods,communicateswiththecontrolplane,managesthePodlife cycle,monitorsthenode’shealth,andmanagesthevolumes.
====================================================================================================

Figure 3: Example of Deployment architecture, with a workload replicated into three Pods. [3] 2.2.2 Pod Architecture InaKubernetessystem,aPodisabasicdeployableunit. Allapplicationsruninsidethe Pod. However, naked Pod usage is not recommended because it can’t be rescheduled in caseofnode failure. Aworkloadobjectis createdto abstractandmanage aPod, and it is easier to manage a Pod using a workload. Workload consists of Deployment, StatefulSet,DaemonSet,JobandCronJob. Deploymentisthemostcommonchoice among the workloads to run stateless applications. A Deployment’s architecture is described Figure 3 with a lower layer of ReplicaSet that keeps a desired number of Pods. For more advanced requirements using persistent data, StatefulSets support the managementofstatefulapplications. JobsandCronJobsareusedtohandleshort-lived tasks. 2.2.3 Service Discovery AsingleclustercanhavealargenumberofPodsrunning. AlthoughthosePodscanbe deployed in different machines (nodes), Kubernetes’s goal is to hide this to the Pods, which don’t need to understand the underlying processes. One of the challenges of servicediscoveryisthesize. Bigcompanies’clustersmayhavethousandsofnodes runningin a cluster. For example, OpenAIruns 7500nodes fora systemof different AImodelssuchasGPT,CLIP,DALL-E[17]. Anotherchallengeistheflexibilityof theworkloads. While eachPodhas itsown IPaddress,it isnotpractical touseit for routingdata. First,PodsareephemeralsinceasinglePodcanbefailed,destroyed,and recreated anytime.

Chunk 1197
source_id: aalto_k8s_security_thesis
page_key: server_api_core_bitstreams_d523d51a_e37d_4df4_acc6_7f3a75f44af1_content

text:
asopposedtoPods,havestableIPaddressesandaloadbalancingfunctiontoPod’s loose coupling with Pod resources. Requests sent to an application would be routed to the service resource and then distributed to the Pod with the container running the application. Pods targeted by a Service are definedusing selectors andlabels. While selectors are defined in Services, labels are attached to Pods. They are both key-value pairs used by Services to identify the correct Pods. As a result, the requests are routed to the Services as a fixed endpoint, which hides the dynamic nature of Pods and their constant changes. 2.2.4 Configuration Data InKubernetes, ConfigMap andSecretaretwoobjectsusedfordataconfiguration. They are basically key-value data that has a decoupled life cycle from the Deployment. The maindifferenceisthatConfigMapsstoreplaintextdatasuchasenvironmentvariables, configurationfilesorconnectionURLs,whileSecretsstoredatainbase64-encoded form. Data in Secret is confidential, such as passwords, API keys, SSH keys, and OAuth. Secretdataisnotencryptedbydefaultintheetcddatabase. Anyonewithpermission toaccesstheAPIcanfetchandmodifythesecretcontent. Alldataisstoredinplain text, butKubernetesoffers some securityoptions to enhance protection forsecrets.
====================================================================================================

TomakedatainaSecretobjectsecure,therequiredstepsfromthemainKubernetes document are: •Enable encryption at rest [3] •Configure RBAC rules with least-privilege access [3] •Limit access to Secrets to specific containers only [3] 2.2.5 Customer Resource and Operators Inadditiontothemaincomponentssuchasworkloads,services, anddata,Kubernetes provides extensions for software running in the cluster. Custom resource is a powerful extensionbesidesthedefaultKubernetesresource[3]. Customerresourcesarecreated usingCustomResourceDefinition (CRD)or AggregatedAPI. CRDallowsdefiningnew types of Kubernetes resources without requiring a new API server, while Aggregated APIsenableservingresourcesfrommultiple,independentAPIservers. Usingboth the Custom Resource Definition and the bound controller is the most familiar method touseanOperator. Thisprovidestheclusterecosystemwithmoreabilities,suchas add-ons, community extensions [3]. Operators or the operator pattern are software extensions that utilize the customer resources for extended behaviors [3]. It creates a higher-level abstraction with functionality. Operators support automation in many tasks including deploying, restoring application state, or choosing a leader in a distributed environment. This is the complicated pattern with hard usage in the Kubernetes ecosystem, but really powerful. Common tools developed in the Kubernetes ecosystem are operator-based, such as Strimzi Kafka and Trivy Operator [18, 19].
====================================================================================================

canbe usedtoimplementa securityframeworkforKubernetes,including defensein depth, the principle of least privilege, and limiting the attack surface [22]. Principle of Least Privilege To limit the damage caused by security, the principle of least privilege demands the most restrictive set of privileges possible to perform a task [24]. Kubernetes components should be granted with the least permissions to function and operate [25]. Therefore, an actor with access to over-granted permissions resources can perform unauthorized actions using these permissions. Combined with the defense in depth principle, there must be several configurationsto limit permissions andreduce privileges in different layers. Limiting the Attack Surface Pratyusaetal. definedattacksurfaceasthesetofwaysinwhichanadversarycanenter the system and potentially cause damage [26]. In Kubernetes, components expose APIs for communication, especially the kube API and the kubelet. Even though these componentsarenotrevealedtotheexternalside,limitingtheattacksurfaceonthem restrains the chance of lateral movement. Moreover, endpoints for sending requests to Pods have also been published. On top of that, more and more access points would be opened as the cluster size grows. Consequently, this reveals more attack vectors into a cluster. Shift Left Security ShiftleftsecuritywasaphilosophyproposedbyLarrySmithin2021. Thisconcept focusesonsecurityprocessesthatareconductedintheearlystagesofdevelopment rather than waiting until the end [27]. Shift left security can be applied in the context ofKubernetesandDevOpsusingbestpracticesineachstagelistedin2.3. Conducting the security processes in the early stage may require effort, but it is necessary because theKubernetessystemismorecomplexthanthetraditionalone. Morespecifically,

thisemphasizestheeffectivenessofdoingsecurityasearlyinthelifecycleaspossible. Forexample,itiseasiertodetectavulnerabilitybystaticallyanalyzingacontainer before its deployment than trying to detect something erroneous at runtime. This approach allows for catching the security problems in the early stages and reduces the management of these issues in later stages [27]. 2.4.3 Security Frameworks Currently, there are several available frameworks for Kubernetes security. The frameworksincludeasetofguidelinesandbestpracticestomanagerisksystematically. Common standard frameworks are the 4C’s model, NIST SP 800-190, and the Kubernetes Hardening Guide from the National Security Agency [3, 28, 5]. The 4C’s model stand for Cloud,Clusters,Containers, andCode. This framework aims to build a security process for these layers. For the Cloud layer, it focuses on infrastructure in Kubernetes, such as the control plane, nodes, etcd, and the API. Moreover, the security of the cloud provider is also a concern. The Cluster layer works on cluster components and the applications running. This layer has a list of areas including authentication, RBAC, network policies, secrets management, and Pod security standards.
====================================================================================================

Figure 4: Threat matrix for Kubernetes [30] are relevant to orchestration security in Kubernetes [30]. This matrix is usable for identifying attack vectors and prioritizing defenses for any Kubernetes system. The main techniques include: •Initial Access: Initial access techniques are attack methods to gain access to the resources. Attackers can gain this access through cloud credentials, compromised images, vulnerable applications, and exposed interfaces. •Execution: Executiontechniquesaremethodsusedtorunmaliciouscodeinside a cluster. Attackers canuse malicious commands, runtheir owncontainer, and run code inside the deployed application. •Persistence: Persistencetechniquesareusedtokeeptheattackerswithaccess to the cluster. Attackers may do it using backdoor containers, hostPath volume mounts, a directory, and Kubernetes CronJobs to run malicious code. •Privilege escalation: These techniques are used by attackers to get higher privileges in the environment than those they currently have [30]. This can bedonebyaccessingaprivilegedcontainerorbyhavinghighpermissionsto resources such as hostPath mounts and cluster-admin bindings. •Defenseevasion: Defenseevasionisatechniqueusedtohidetheactivitiesof attackers. Evidence such as container logs and Kubernetes events is cleared. AttackerscanalsocreatesimilarlynamedbackdoorPodsthatlookidenticalto Kubernetes’ main components. •Credentialaccess: Attackersusecredentialaccesstechniquestostealcredentials, includingKubernetesSecrets,serviceaccounts,andcredentialsinconfiguration files.

TheCVEpublicdatabasecouldbeeffectivelyusedtoaddresssecurity risks and reduce the attack surface of a Kubernetes cluster.

3 Literature Review ThischapterprovidesaliteraturereviewonKubernetesclustersecuritybestpractices. Theliteraturereviewisstructuredtoanalyzethebestpracticesindiverselayersand components. Section 3.1 explores approaches for protecting a typical Kubernetes cluster. Section 3.2 delves deeper into safeguarding the workload and pod, which represent the core of the application. In the lower layer of workload, section 3.3 covers techniques to make Docker images and containerized applications safe for usage. Section 3.4 displays how tomonitor and what to monitor for eachcomponent and each layer. Section 3.5 focuses on compliance checks and methods to protect components that operate the system, such as control plane components and worker node components. Finally, section 3.6 represents the current gap of these practices and what is missing for a team to apply in software development. 3.1 Cluster Security At the highest cluster level, certain practices can influence the entire cluster. This sectionoutlines variouspracticesthatallcontributeto creatingasecurecluster. These practices include secret management, authentication, authorization, and managing namespace resource quotas. Typically, these tasks fall under the responsibilities of the cluster administrator. 3.1.1 Namespace Boundaries Kubernetesnamespacesaremeanttoseparateresourcesformultipleteamswithina cluster.

anditisnotencryptedwithoutconfiguration[3]. Thisisacommontargetintheattack vector. This subsection discusses methods to work with secret and secure sensitive information. Secret Encryption And Secret Storage Encryptionistheprimarychoicetoprotectsecretvalues. Arshetal. studyexplains thatusingcryptographyandencryptionisamainwaytosecuredataonthecloud[32]. InKubernetes,secretsareinsecurelystoredintheetcddatabaseinbase64encoding by default. It can be encrypted at rest and in transit [3]. Besides the etcd database, secret values can be stored in third-party solutions such as HashiCorp Vault. This third-party tool supports secret storage with strong encryption for data at rest, in use, and in transit, and offers many advanced features such as auto-rotating secrets and dynamic secrets. Secret Rotation And Revocation In secret management, secret rotation is the process of regularly updating the secret. Secret rotation is an important practice to protect sensitive data. A secret can be safe from human guessing, but not safe from machine attacks. Shortening the period of a secretmakesbruteforceattacksharderandmorecomplex. Eventhoughthesecretsare compromised, threat actors only have a limited time to perform unauthorized actions using the leaked key.

For example, after leaving the organization, an employee can’t use the existing secret anymore. Apart from the main methods for secrets protection using key and encryption, Shazibul et al. also recommend spreading sensitive workload to prevent escalation on a compromised node [33]. While authentication is used to verify the identity of the actor, authorization is later used to verify the permission associated with the request [16]. In Kubernetes, the core component that exposes the API and verifies all requests is the kube-apiserver [16]. Authentication Authentication is the initial step in Kubernetes security, verifying actors, services or applications that are trying to access the API. There are many mechanisms for

authentication, including static tokens, basic authentication with a password, service account tokens, bootstrap tokens, webhook tokens, authentication proxy tokens, client certificates, and user impersonation [16]. All methods can be enabled simultaneously. Authorization Afterauthenticationistheauthorizationprocess. Theauthorizationmoduledetermines the permission of the identified user or service and returns the decision to allow or deny. There are many authorization modes, such as Node authorization, ABAC mode, RBAC, AlwaysDeny, AlwaysAllow, and webhooks [22]. Each option has its own pros andcons, butRBACis themostwidelyusedandrecommendeddespiteits complexity [25,35]. AlwaysDenymodeblocksalltherequests,andAlwaysAllowmodeallowsall requests. Theyshouldbeusedonlyfortestinganddevelopment, notforthereleased production. 3.1.4 Role-based Access Control Role-based access control(RBAC) is an authorization method that works based on the roles of users [25]. Roles with a set of permissions are created and assigned to users. The scopes of using RBAC are cluster-wide and namespace-wide [22]. The four core objects used for RBAC are Roles, ClusterRoles, RoleBindings, and ClusterRoleBindings.
====================================================================================================

source_id: aalto_k8s_security_thesis

There are use cases to use these objects: •Use Roles and RoleBindings to create roles and assign them to users in the namespace scope. •Reuse role with ClusterRoles in different namespaces and assign to users using RoleBindings. •UseClusterRolestocreatecluster-widepermissionsandassigntousersusing ClusterRoleBindings [22]. 3.1.5 Network Policies In the Kubernetes environment, the network model is flat, and all traffic is allowed in theclusternetwork. Thismeansthateverypodcancommunicatewithanyotherpodin the cluster freely, even in different namespaces. This approach simplifies the network design in the cluster [25]. Network policies have been introduced to control the traffic betweenpods. Networkpoliciesareresourcesthatdefinehowtrafficisroutedinthe network. They control what pods can send or receive requests. Ingress and egress rules are the rules used to control the traffic. Ingress defines rules for incoming traffic, and egress defines rules for outgoing traffic. Consequently, thepodingresstrafficofthedestinationmustmatchtheegresstrafficofthesource. Thebestpracticesrecommendapplyingthenetworkpoliciestoallpods. Itisriskyfor teamstodesignnetworkpoliciesbutaccidentallyforgetdetailsandleaveitunsecured. Agoodpracticehereisto defaultdenypolicy [25]. Consequently, alltrafficisblocked until new policies are created for the pod.
====================================================================================================

3.1.7 Dynamic Admission Control DynamicAdmissionControlisacomponentinKubernetesthatinterceptsrequests to the API server. It has an advanced function to allow customized configurations without impact on the kube-apiserver [3]. Dynamic admission control, implemented via admission webhooks, significantly strengthens Kubernetes security by providing a flexible andproactive mechanismfor enforcingcustom securitypoliciesat thepoint ofresourcecreationormodification. Itcansafeguardclusterresourcesbothduring runtimeandatdeployment. Intheruntimephase,theadmissioncontrollerprevents unauthorized access and configuration changes. Unlike built-in controllers, it can becustomizedtoaddressspecificsecuritythreats[42]. Duringpoddeployment,the dynamic admission controller can reject any deployments that violate established policies. It can be integrated into the CI/CD pipeline, aligning with modern software development processes and integrating with image scanning tools to prevent the deployment of vulnerable images [43]. 3.2 Workload and Pod Level Security Workloads or pods are the most fine-grained means to run the main business logic of the application. Therefore, they should be taken care of. This subsection discusses security issues related to configuration to protect a single workload running in the cluster.
====================================================================================================

3.2.1 Pod Security Standards and Pod Security Admission Securing configurations in Kubernetes workload presents significant challenges. An improperly configured pod may be vulnerable to exploitation, which could potentially compromisetheintegrityoftheentirecluster. PodSecurityStandards(PSSs)makethe security configuration painless and efficient. PSSs are Kubernetes policies for pods to ensure that all pods in the cluster adhere to consistent security baselines, minimizing vulnerabilitieswhilemaintainingoperationalflexibilitywithdifferentlevels. ThePSA enforces these policies. Three levels of Pod Security Standards are: •Privileged: Openpolicyprovidingthewidestlevelofpermissions. Itpermits some privilege escalations. [3]. •Baseline: Policythatminimallyrestrictsandcontrolssomeprivilegeescalations. It permits the default configuration for Pods with minimal specifications. [3]. •Restricted: Themostrestrictedone,usinghardeningbestpracticesforPods[3]. Pod Security Admission (PSA) is a built-in admission controller embedded in the kube-apiserver that enforces the PSS. If a request violates the policies defined in the PSS, PSA will react in 3 different modes: •enforce: Policy violations result in the rejection of the pod. [3].
====================================================================================================

•audit: Policy violations lead to an audit annotation being added to the event recorded in the audit log, while they are still permitted. [3]. •warn: Policy violations result in a warning for the user. However, they are generally permitted. [3]. PSS and PSA work together as a two-part system. PSS provides the policy framework, while the PSA module operates it through namespace labels and real-time validationofpodcreationandupdateperiods. ThisduoisKubernetes’primarypod securitymechanism,ensuringclustersaresecure,flexible,andeasytomanage. An admin labels a namespace with a PSS level. When a pod is created, PSA reads the namespace label, fetches the corresponding PSS policy, and validates the pod’s spec. Depending on the configured policies and PSA mode set, PSA may reject, log data, or trigger an alert. 3.2.2 Deploy Honeypot Workload and Intrusion Detection Systems Canary deployment is a strategy to deploy multiple versions of an application and routeasmallamountoftrafficfromasmallusersubsettothenewpodversionsbefore fully rolling out. This technique can be applied in the Kubernetes environment. Fake workloads and services are deployed in the cluster.

3.2.4 Resource Management for Pods According to Eunsook et al., CPU-intensive pods lead to degradation of cluster performance [48]. The same research shows a decrease in half of the CPU utilization. When a newpod is created, the kube-schedulermonitors the resources of the worker nodes and tries to assign the new pod to one of them. It also points to another root cause of high CPU usage, which is that network workload consumes and raises a researchquestionforfurtherresourcemanagementofKubernetes. Inanotherresearch, Tan et al. created the AlloX scheduler using Go andPython to optimize the dynamic fair allocation of CPU and GPU resources on a Kubernetes cluster for machine learning jobs [49]. Zhang et al. designed a scheduling algorithm, CloudSim, which is claimed to be better than the original scheduling algorithm and reduces cost [50]. For developers at the moment, resource requests and limits allow a simpler and default way of allocation and limitation of resources to Kubernetes objects.
====================================================================================================

Image Scanning in deployment phase Evenifimagescanningisdoneduringdevelopment,itcanbeactivatedinnumerous stages, especially with the adoption of DevOps. New vulnerabilities can be found during deployment scanning, even if they pass the first scanning in development. It is the best practice to scan images in multiple stages of software development. TheValidatingAdmissionWebhook is an admission controller designed to support validating requests to the kube-apiserver. It checks images belonging to workloads thatarereadyfordeployment. Iftheimagesdonotmeetthedefinedsecurityscanning requirements, the workload will not be accepted. Sysdighasdevelopedanopen-sourceprojectcalled AnchoreEngine tofacilitatethis process. Here’showitworksinconjunctionwiththeValidatingAdmissionWebhook [16]: •A request to create a workload is transmitted to the kube-apiserver and then forwardedtoValidatingAdmissionWebhook. Itwilllaterbeprocessedbythe Sysdig scanning tool. •The scanning tool evaluates the image and returns the results to the kubeapiserver. •Basedonthescanningresults,thekube-apiservermakesthefinaldecisionto either approve or reject the workload creation request. •In summary, the decision to reject or approve the workload lies with the kube-apiserver, guided by the results from the scanning tool.

Image Scanning at the runtime stage Afterbeingdeployedtothecluster,imagesarefrequentlyre-scannedbythescannerto checkfornewvulnerabilitiesinthedatabase. Thisisasandbox-basedsolutionthatutilizes machine learning and smart algorithms to detect vulnerabilities in running containers. It tracks the network traffic, processes, system calls, memory, and other behaviors. Furthermore,filesystemsarecheckedtodetectmalwareandthreats[25]. Thismethod isgaining popularitywithsolutionsdeliveredin themarket,suchas AquaSecurity’s Dynamic Threat Analysis, Google’s Container Threat Detection, and Sysdig Falco [53, 54, 55].

3.3.4 LimitRanger LimitRanger is a built-in admission controller, and LimitRange is an object in Kubernetes. LimitRangeisresponsibleforsettingminimum,maximum,anddefault resource limits (like CPU and memory) for containers and pods within a namespace. LimitRanger then verifies and rejects any request that violates the constraints [25] in LimitRange. Violated request will receive a 403 status code response [16]. The example13configurationbelowshowsthesettingoftheminimumof0.05CPUcores, maximumof0.5CPUcores,andmemorylimitof512mebibytesandminimum50 mebibytes of memory for the namespace demo. 3.4 Monitoring, Alerting and Auditing Whena Kubernetesclusteris running,itis theperfecttarget foranattack. Therefore should be strategies to protect it during the running time. Most of the configurations mentioned before protect the cluster from threats while it runs. Moreover, it can be pushed further with protection during runtime. This section discusses the three mostimportantstrategies in theruntime phaseofthe Kubernetes cluster, including monitoring the system, alerting errors and audit logs. Monitoring MonitoringintheKubernetessysteminvolvesmeasurementsandmetricsusedtocollect real-timedataaboutclusterhealthandresourceutilization, toalert[25]. Datausedfor monitoring are pods logs, network logs, application flow logs, and metrics that should bemonitoredareconnectionpersecond,datapersecond,requestpersecond,CPU,and memoryusage. Thisenablesproactivedetectionofbottlenecks,faileddeployments, and scaling needs, ensuring optimal operations and quick troubleshooting.

3.5 Compliance Check Compliance validation provides systematic verification that the Kubernetes system adheres to organizational policies and industry security standards. This process involves continuous auditing of cluster configurations against established benchmarks to identify deviations from security baselines. For Kubernetes environments, the CIS KubernetesBenchmark releasedbytheCentreforInternetSecurityandthe Kubernetes Hardening Guide by the National Security Agency are 2 ongoing industry standards. Adaptingtothesedocumentsisalsoabestpracticethatneedstobedoneduringthe development process. For a project using Kubernetes as an orchestration system, there could be a compliance team that is responsible forensuring compliance checks are adopted by the team and the software [25]. Therefore, following the shift left security principle, using these compliance documents during the development stage is an excellent choice to remove security risks in the early stage. 3.5.1 CIS Security Benchmark The CIS Kubernetes Benchmark [60], developed by the Centre for Internet Security, delivers prescriptive configuration guidelines for hardening Kubernetes clusters. It givessecurityrecommendationsforthedirectconfigurationofKubernetesresource components in the worker nodes and control plane.

Structured around five critical assessment domains, it addresses: •Control plane elements with API server, scheduler, controller manager [60] •Etcd configuration [60] •Control plane configuration [60] •Worker node components (kubelet and kube-proxy) [60] •Kubernetes policy enforcement [60] Allofthesecomponentsareinherentlypartofthecluster,andanycompromisecan leadtotheentireclusterbeingattacked. ThechecklistprovidedbytheCISBenchmark offers recommendations for hardening the resource components in the Kubernetes worker nodes and control plane. By using this configuration, teams have strong security settings on the available components in the Kubernetes architecture. Each iteminthechecklistreturnsdetailsaboutthedefaultconfigurationvalue,rationale, impact, audit, and remediation [60]. 3.5.2 Kubernetes Hardening Guide IncontrasttotheconcentrationinKubernetescontrolplaneandworkernoderesources. TheKubernetesHardeningGuidedocument[5],developedbytheNSAandCISA, spreads the checklist across all practices of Kubernetes. It encompasses a broad range ofKubernetespractices,includingpodsecurity,networkconfiguration,authentication, auditing, and vulnerability management. This broad scope ensures that organizations

Amitetal. haveconductedacomprehensive study to show compliance models of 13 categories related to risk including: •"Data encryption, key management" [61] •"Media protection" [61] •"Identification, authentication and authorization" [61] •"Virtualization and resource abstraction" [61] •"Portability and interoperability" [61] •"Application security" [61] •"Security risk assessment and management" [61] •"Privacy, electronic discovery and other legal issues" [61] •"Contingency planning" [61] •"Data center operations, maintenance" [61] •"Incident response" [61] •"Compliance, audit and accountability" [61] •"Awareness and training" [61] In the cloud industry, cloud organizations have their own compliance solution. They workforcomplianceenforcementandcheckingincontainersandinfrastructure. All these listed solutions by the organizations provide support for runtime compliance checking and the configuration of custom rules [68].

3.6 Research Gaps SecuritypracticeshavebeenimprovedineachversionbytheKubernetesteamandthe securityteam. Newvulnerabilitieshavebeenreportedandrecorded,andnewtools used to harden Kubernetes clusters have been invented in the last few years. However, these works have also revealed some challenges and gaps: •There is no default secure configuration. The main document page from the Kubernetes team also provides configuration tips with simple and minimal configurationtoreduceerroroccurrence[3]. Thisincreasestheattackvector for inexperienced developers. •There is no systematic approach for security when building and deploying Kubernetesapplications. ThereareguidelinessuchastheKubernetesHardening GuidebytheNationalSecurityAgency,theCISKubernetesBenchmark,and the main Kubernetes security document [5, 60, 3]. However, when applying for a software product, these guides create overhead and may slow down the developmentprocess. Thereisaneed forclearinstructionsonhowto securea cluster for developers and system administrators. •A complex architecture of Kubernetes makes it extremely hard to build a comprehensive supply chain security. All the mentioned guides cannot propose afriendlyend-to-endsupplychainsecurityapproachforadevelopmentprocess. Thereshouldbeamethodtobuildbothefficientandsecuresoftwaredevelopment. The above research gaps highlight a need for a different security approach for developers and system administrators. This thesis primarily addresses these gaps and createsanewformalapproachforbuildingasecureKubernetescluster. Thisapproach neededtobecapableofbeingusedbydevelopersinthedevelopmentprocessingeneral cases. There aredifferent cloudKubernetes servicessuch asGKE, EKS, AKS,IKS, ACK,andon-premisesenvironments. Thenewhigh-levelapproachshouldworkfor all cases. In addition, this approach needs to translate high-level recommendations to somethingthatusersanddeveloperscaneasilyapplytothesystemwithoutslowing down the release cycles and collaboration process of teams.
====================================================================================================

4 Methodology The primary goal for a new method is to provide a comprehensive approach for Kubernetessecurity. Thisframework’stargetistocoverthecurrentgapsinthesecurity oftheKubernetessystemandotherframeworks. Thissectionisstructuredtointroduce the ideas and instructions to apply, and how the proposed method bridges the current gaps in Kubernetes security. Section 4.1 discusses the overview of a new method. Section 4.2 explores the details of the main methods and displays their structure. Section 4.3 finally shows how it can be applied by developers in a real system. 4.1 Methodology Overview Some Kubernetes security frameworks have been introduced in Security Frameworks, includingthe4C’sModel,NISTSP800-190,andKubernetesHardeningGuidance. However,theseframeworksjustgivealistofbestpracticesandrecommendations,and it is still far away from a comprehensive approach with fine-grained instructions for a Kubernetessystem. Thissectionintroducesanewapproachforusingmatrix-based Kubernetes security. An initial version of this matrix security is shown in Table 3. Kubernetes clusters and Docker containers are both included in the scope of this new approach, which is a difference from the frameworks introduced earlier. The scope islimitedonthesetechnologiesbecausetheyarethestandardindustrialonesatthe moment. There are other alternatives used in the market for containerisation and orchestration,suchasDockerSwarmandOpenShift.
====================================================================================================
